text: The video shows a patient with a 10 by 10 centimeter incisional hernia located in the upper midline near the xiphoid process.
The surgeon performs an ETEP incisional hernia repair with surgical ports positioned medial to the right linea semilunaris.
The camera focuses on the lower portion of the abdomen, specifically the umbilical area, with the left rectus muscle visible on the far side.
The surgeon has partially transected the upper posterior rectus sheath, revealing the underlying anatomical structures in the upper midline region near the xiphoid.
The cut edge of the posterior rectus sheath is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon grasps the posterior rectus sheath, with the rectus muscle visible above in the lower portion of the abdomen near the umbilical area.
The surgeon begins dissecting the preperitoneal fat from the posterior rectus sheath in the lower abdominal region, using careful separation techniques to expose the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon attempts to perform a transversus abdominis release (TAR) to access the subdiaphragmatic space, aiming to overlap the mesh beneath the diaphragm.
The surgeon points out the usual TAR plane located behind the transverse abdominis muscle, indicating a fascial plane behind the transverse abdominis that will be addressed later.
The surgeon identifies the scar from a previous left nephrectomy, visible as a chevron-shaped incision on the abdominal surface.
The surgeon attempts to locate the TAR plane, aiming to position beneath the transverse abdominis muscle.
The transverse abdominis muscle becomes visible as the surgeon continues the dissection.
The surgical field shows significant scar tissue resulting from the previous left nephrectomy chevron incision.
The surgeon identifies the tar plane located directly behind the transverse abdominis muscle, highlighting the anatomical relationship between these structures.
The surgeon identifies the plane beneath the transverse abdominis and proceeds to dissect towards the diaphragm area, navigating through the scar tissue from a previous incision.
The surgeon identifies the transition area where the transverse abdominus muscle detaches from the costal margin, and the diaphragm meets the transverse abdominus at this junction.
The surgeon identifies a clear plane located deep to the diaphragm muscle, distinguishing it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon navigates into the areolar plane adjacent to the diaphragm muscle, similar to the plane near the transverse abdominis.
The transverse abdominis muscle is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon is positioned behind the fibrous part of the diaphragm, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates medially towards the xiphoid process, encountering a more complex anatomical area.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers to stay close to the diaphragm muscle fibers, maintaining proximity to the fibrous part of the diaphragm.
The surgeon moves medially and prepares to divide the visible fibrous layer of the diaphragm.
The surgeon encounters an additional fibrous layer behind the previously dissected tissue, requiring careful navigation.
The surgeon identifies and refers to a distinct anatomical layer as the 'shelf of the diaphragm' during the procedure.
The surgeon maneuvers deep to the diaphragm's shelf, attempting to maintain a position behind this anatomical layer.

The surgeon performs an upper tar on the patient's right side, dividing the posterior rectus sheath, which includes the posterior lamella of the internal oblique, transversus abdominis, and diaphragm contribution to the posterior sheath.
The surgeon identifies a piece of fat as the xiphoid process, highlighting its location and appearance within the surgical field.
The surgeon cuts through the fat behind the xiphoid process, creating an opening into a natural anatomical space.
The surgeon stops cutting through the fat and closely follows the sternal fibers of the xiphoid process, carefully navigating around this anatomical structure.
The muscle fibers of the xiphoid are visible behind the xiphoid process.
The surgeon identifies a clear anatomical plane, with a visible shelf-like structure on the right side of the screen.
The surgeon navigates behind the fibrous portion of the diaphragm, referred to as the shelf, ensuring to maintain the position behind this structure during the procedure.
The surgeon attempts to find a plane behind the fibrous shelf of the diaphragm, navigating through the visible anatomical structures.
The surgeon splits the fibers of the diaphragm, focusing on the right side of the screen where a fibrous shelf is visible.
The surgeon divides the fibrous shelf of the diaphragm, carefully manipulating the tissue to separate it.
The surgical field shows adhesions with sticky tissue, making dissection challenging.
The surgeon identifies muscle fibers and plans to stay deep to them during the dissection.
The surgeon carefully dissects the area, ensuring that the floor is free of muscle tissue while navigating through the sticky surgical field.
The surgeon identifies muscle on the floor, indicating an anterior position to the diaphragm, and prepares to take the diaphragm down.
The surgeon attempts to connect the dissected space on the left side with the space on the right side, navigating between visible anatomical structures.
The surgeon cuts through a fibrous layer, revealing underlying tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully enters the subdiaphragmatic space, ensuring no diaphragm fibers are brought down during the dissection.
The surgeon highlights the fibrous portion where the diaphragm contributes to the posterior rectus sheath, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon has completed the dissection of the left subdiaphragmatic space, revealing the anatomical area beneath the diaphragm.
The surgeon maneuvers behind the xiphoid process, carefully navigating the anatomical space without disturbing surrounding structures.
The surgeon attempts to stay deep to the anatomical shelf, maintaining careful positioning within the dissected subdiaphragmatic space.
The surgeon positions the instrument under the diaphragm, maintaining close proximity to the diaphragm muscle during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies and carefully controls multiple small vessels entering the diaphragm muscle to prevent retraction and bleeding, ensuring effective cauterization.
The surgeon completes the partial tear in the right upper quadrant, focusing on the area beneath the diaphragm muscle.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the instrument to stay close to the diaphragm muscle, maintaining proximity to the anatomical shelf visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon navigates the area behind the xiphoid, staying between the shelf and sternal fibers. As the dissection progresses to the left and right sides, the surgeon transitions deeper to avoid disrupting the costal fibers of the diaphragm.
The surgeon transects the posterior rectus sheath, cutting through the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and the transversus abdominis muscles.
The surgeon identifies the fibrous layer of the diaphragm, which contributes to the posterior rectus sheath, located deep to the current dissection plane.
The surgeon remains deep to the anatomical shelf, mindful of its presence while continuing the dissection.
The surgeon maintains a deep position relative to the anatomical shelf, ensuring careful navigation to avoid disrupting surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies two distinct layers located posterior to the transverse abdominis muscle in the surgical field.
The surgeon cuts through the fibrous layer and the diaphragm shelf, visibly transecting these structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to dissect deep to the diaphragm shelf on the patient's right side, maintaining the surgical plane beneath the fibrous layer.
The surgeon attempts to separate the peritoneum from the underlying structures, but it adheres tightly and does not easily detach.
The surgeon applies traction to the peritoneum, carefully separating it from underlying structures using anatomical knowledge.
The surgeon navigates behind the diaphragm, positioning deep to the diaphragm shelf, as visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon positions the instruments right up against the diaphragm muscle, maintaining proximity to the anatomical structure during the procedure.
The upper edge of the fascial defect is visible, indicating the boundary of the area being addressed in the procedure.
The surgeon tucks the mesh well under the diaphragm, ensuring good overlap in the cephalad direction.
